questions.question: In the Google 'technical debt' framing of ML systems, what is the trained model relative to a real production ML system?
options: The bulk of the system, with serving and monitoring as minor add-ons
A tiny box dwarfed by config, data collection, feature extraction, serving, and monitoring
Roughly half the system, balanced against the data pipeline alone
Largely irrelevant once the system has reached production
questions.explanation: ML code is a tiny sliver of a real system; the surrounding plumbing (data, features, serving, monitoring) is the bulk and where most failures originate. The model is not the majority of the work.

questions.question: What single feature most distinguishes the MLOps lifecycle from classic software delivery?
options: It uses containers to package the application
It runs unit tests in continuous integration
A return edge from monitoring back to data, so the system is designed to be retrained rather than shipped once
It deploys behind a load balancer with multiple replicas
questions.explanation: The defining feature of MLOps is the closed loop: monitoring detects drift/decay and feeds fresh data back to retraining. Containers, unit tests, and load balancers are shared with ordinary DevOps and do not make it a loop.

questions.question: To reproduce an exact model, a model is best understood as a function of which inputs?
options: Code only — version control of the source is sufficient
Code and data only — config is incidental to the result
Code, data, configuration (hyperparameters), and the random seed
Data and hyperparameters only — code is regenerated from them
questions.explanation: A model is a function of (code + data + config + random seed), so reproducibility requires capturing every one. Versioning code alone leaves the data and hyperparameters unpinned.

questions.question: Loading (unpickling) a model file from an untrusted source is safe because pickle only deserializes data, not executable code.
questions.explanation: False. Loading a pickle executes arbitrary code, so you must never unpickle a model from an untrusted source; treat untrusted artifacts as code and prefer ONNX/SavedModel or a signed, provenance-checked artifact.

questions.question: Which statement best captures the main trade-off of batch (offline) serving?
options: Low per-request latency, but predictions can become stale over time
High throughput and simplicity, but predictions can be stale (hours old)
Always-on, per-request freshness, but a scalable service to operate
Near-real-time per-event reaction without any synchronous request
questions.explanation: Batch serving runs inference on a schedule over a large dataset and stores results: simple and high-throughput with no latency pressure, but the cost is staleness. Per-request freshness and always-on operation describe online serving; per-event reaction describes streaming.

questions.code:
-- training pipeline (nightly batch)
SELECT AVG(amount) AS avg_purchase
FROM purchases
WHERE user_id = :uid AND ts >= NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days';

# serving path (application code, Python)
rows = recent_purchases(uid, days=7)   # note: 7-day window
avg_purchase = mean(r.amount for r in rows) if rows else 0.0
questions.question: A model has excellent offline accuracy on its test set but degraded live accuracy. The feature `avg_purchase` is computed by the training SQL and the serving code shown below. Why does offline evaluation fail to catch the problem?
options: Offline evaluation uses too small a test set to detect the difference
Offline evaluation uses the training-side computation, never exercising the divergent serving code
The model overfits the test set, which masks the serving-side bug
Concept drift has changed P(y|x) since the test set was created
questions.explanation: This is training/serving skew. Offline evaluation uses the training-side computation, so it cannot see a divergence that only exists in the serving path. It is not overfitting, test-set size, or concept drift — the input→output relationship is unchanged; only the feature computation differs.

questions.question: How does a feature store eliminate training/serving skew?
options: It retrains the model more frequently so serving values stay current
It computes each feature from one definition, serving it to both the offline (training) and online (serving) stores
It caches serving predictions so repeated inputs return identical answers
It converts all features to ONNX so they become framework-independent
questions.explanation: A feature store derives both training and serving values from one definition, so train and serve see identical values. Frequent retraining, prediction caching, and ONNX conversion do nothing to unify how a feature is computed across the two paths.

questions.question: A six-month-old churn model still passes its original offline test set, and its input features are statistically unchanged, yet live predictions are clearly worse. What is the most likely cause?
options: Data drift — the input distribution P(x) has shifted
Concept drift — P(y|x) has changed while inputs look the same
Training/serving skew in the feature pipeline
A data-quality bug such as a surge of nulls
questions.explanation: Unchanged inputs but worse predictions point to concept drift: P(y|x) has shifted, so the same profiles now imply a different outcome and the stale test set cannot reveal it. Data drift would show a changed input distribution; skew and null surges would also alter inputs or feature values.

questions.question: Using the Population Stability Index, a feature has reference proportions [0.50, 0.30, 0.15, 0.05] and live proportions [0.40, 0.35, 0.20, 0.05] across four buckets. What is the approximate PSI and the verdict, given PSI per bucket = (live − ref)·ln(live/ref)?
options: ≈0.044 — stable, no significant drift
≈0.150 — moderate shift, watch the feature
≈0.245 — major shift, raise a drift alert
≈0.000 — the distributions are identical
questions.explanation: The per-bucket terms are 0.0223 + 0.0077 + 0.0144 + 0 ≈ 0.0444, which is below the 0.1 threshold, so the feature is stable. The distributions are clearly not identical (so not 0), and the value does not reach the moderate (0.1–0.25) or major (>0.25) bands.

questions.question: Ground-truth labels for a loan-default model arrive months after each prediction. Which signals can you monitor in the meantime as early proxies for trouble? (Select all that apply.)
options: Input feature drift via PSI or the Kolmogorov–Smirnov test
Live AUC/F1 computed against same-day ground-truth labels
Prediction (output) distribution shift
Data-quality checks: nulls, out-of-range values, schema changes
questions.explanation: With delayed labels you rely on label-free proxies: input drift, prediction-distribution shift, and data-quality checks. Computing live AUC/F1 requires ground-truth labels, which by assumption do not exist yet, so that option is impossible here.

questions.question: For a high-throughput **internal** model service where latency and payload size matter, gRPC (HTTP/2 + binary Protobuf) is generally preferred over REST/JSON, while REST/JSON's strengths are universality and easy debugging.
questions.explanation: True. gRPC's HTTP/2 and binary Protobuf give lower latency and smaller payloads, preferred for high-throughput internal services; REST/JSON wins on universality and debuggability, not raw performance.

questions.question: A nightly CT pipeline retrained a model and auto-deployed it, but the new model was significantly worse than the previous one. What is missing from the pipeline?
options: A larger training dataset to improve the retrain
An evaluation gate that promotes a model only if it beats production (and baseline) meaningfully
A faster GPU so retraining completes before deployment
More frequent retraining to overwrite the bad model sooner
questions.explanation: CT must re-run the full offline evaluation and promote only if the new model beats production and the baseline; 'training finished' is not 'model is good.' Bigger data, faster hardware, or more frequent retrains do not stop a worse model from shipping without a gate.

questions.question: Which statements about safe-rollout strategies are accurate? (Select all that apply.)
options: Shadow deployment sends mirrored traffic to the new model but does not use its output, giving zero user risk
A/B testing is primarily for comparing a business outcome (e.g. click-through) with statistical significance
Blue-green deployment exposes the new model gradually to a slowly ramping slice of traffic
Canary release routes a small slice of real traffic and ramps up if the model stays healthy
questions.explanation: Shadow ignores outputs (zero risk), A/B compares a business metric with significance, and canary routes a small ramping slice. Blue-green is an all-at-once full switch with instant rollback — not a gradual ramp; that description belongs to canary.

questions.question: A team shadow-deploys a new ranking model: it receives a mirror of live traffic and its predictions are logged but never served. Which limitation is inherent to this strategy?
options: It exposes a small percentage of users to a potentially bad model
It produces no business-metric signal, since its outputs never affect users
It cannot validate the new model's latency under real traffic
It requires an instant all-or-nothing traffic switch
questions.explanation: Because shadow outputs are never used, you get real-world behavior and latency validation at zero user risk but no business signal (no clicks/conversions attributable to it). It does exercise latency, exposes no users, and is not an all-or-nothing switch (that is blue-green).

questions.question: A recommender's own predictions shape what users see, and those interactions become the next training set. What risk does this create, and what is the suggested mitigation?
options: Training/serving skew; fix it with a feature store
Self-reinforcing bias, where the model amplifies its own predictions; mitigate by logging to detect it and injecting exploration/randomization
Concept drift; mitigate by retraining on a fixed schedule
Data leakage; mitigate by removing the target column from features
questions.explanation: When a model's predictions influence its future training data, it can reinforce its own biases; mitigate by logging enough to detect this and injecting exploration/randomization. Skew, concept drift, and leakage are distinct problems with different causes and fixes.

questions.question: Which claims about scaling and latency for an online model service are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Tail latency (p95/p99) matters more than the average for user experience
Replicas must hold per-request state so the load balancer can route correctly
Request batching raises throughput by amortizing GPU overhead but adds a little latency
The latency budget should cover the whole path — feature lookups, network hops, and cold starts — not just model compute
questions.explanation: p95/p99 matters more than the mean, batching trades a little latency for throughput, and the budget must measure the entire path. Statelessness — not per-request state — is exactly what makes replicas interchangeable behind a load balancer, so that option is wrong.

questions.question: Given this PSI helper and the thresholds (<0.1 stable, 0.1–0.25 moderate, >0.25 major), what does the function return for the inputs shown in the comment, and is it correct?
questions.code:
def psi(ref, live):  # ref/live: lists of bucket proportions
    total = 0.0
    for r, l in zip(ref, live):
        total += (l - r) * math.log(l / r)
    return total

# ref  = [0.40, 0.32, 0.20, 0.08]
# live = [0.24, 0.26, 0.30, 0.20]
verdict = psi(ref, live)
options: ≈0.245, correctly flagging a moderate-to-major shift
≈0.044, indicating the feature is stable
It raises a ZeroDivisionError because one bucket proportion is 0
≈0.10, exactly on the stable/moderate boundary
questions.explanation: Summing (live−ref)·ln(live/ref) over the four buckets gives 0.0818 + 0.0125 + 0.0405 + 0.1100 ≈ 0.245, which crosses the moderate line and triggers an alert. No bucket proportion is 0, so there is no division error, and the value is far above 0.044/0.10.

questions.question: When an LLM-powered system is built on a hosted model API you do not train, what is the key change to how you version and deploy?
options: The model weights become the primary versioned artifact, re-pushed on each change
The prompt template becomes the artifact that changes behavior, versioned, tested, and rollback-able like code
Versioning becomes unnecessary because the provider guarantees deterministic outputs
The feature store replaces the prompt as the unit of deployment
questions.explanation: With a fixed hosted model, the prompt template is what changes behavior, so a prompt edit is a deployment to be versioned, tested, and rolled back. You do not control the provider's weights, outputs are nondeterministic (not deterministic), and feature stores are not the LLM deployment unit.
